summary: Office management and facilities-services marketplace acquired by WeWork; operations were wound down as WeWork retrenched during its own crisis.
lesson: An acquisition by a troubled parent company can doom an otherwise-functioning business.

name: MannKind Corporation
summary: Inhaled insulin product Afrezza faced multiple near-bankruptcies before winning approval, but adoption remained tiny for years against injectable insulin's entrenched market position.
lesson: A better delivery mechanism doesn't overcome physician and patient inertia around an established standard of care.

name: ManyPets (US)
summary: UK-founded pet insurer exited the US market and cut staff after underwriting losses from medical cost inflation made American expansion unsustainable.
lesson: International insurance expansion can outrun a carrier's ability to price risk accurately in a new market.

name: Maple
summary: NYC-based prepared meal delivery startup co-founded by a celebrity chef shut down in 2017, with its team acquired by Deliveroo.
lesson: A crowded meal-delivery market punishes startups that can't reach delivery density fast enough.

name: MapR
summary: Hadoop distribution vendor that raised hundreds of millions but lost ground to Cloudera/Hortonworks and cloud-native data platforms, eventually selling its assets to HPE in a fire sale.
lesson: Being one of three well-funded competitors chasing the same enterprise market left little room for anyone but the leader to survive.

name: MasterClass
summary: Celebrity-taught online course platform saw sharp subscriber growth during the pandemic reverse afterward, leading to layoffs and a roughly 75% internal valuation markdown.
lesson: Pandemic-boosted consumer subscription demand for enrichment content proved largely temporary.

name: Maven
summary: General Motors' car-sharing brand operating in over a dozen US cities was shut down in 2019 as GM redirected investment toward Cruise's autonomous vehicle efforts.
lesson: Corporate mobility experiments get cut quickly when a flashier bet (like autonomy) captures leadership's attention.

name: MedMen
summary: Cannabis retail chain that branded itself as the 'Apple Store of weed' expanded aggressively across multiple states, then saw its stock collapse over 99% and default on debt.
lesson: Aggressive multi-state retail expansion can collapse fast when unit economics never catch up to store count.

name: Meerkat
summary: The live video-streaming app rode SXSW buzz but was crushed within weeks after Twitter cut off its social-graph access and launched rival Periscope.
lesson: Building a growth engine on top of a rival platform's API leaves you exposed to a single policy change.

name: Melinta Therapeutics
summary: Antibiotic maker with four approved drugs filed Chapter 11 in 2020 after revenue failed to cover debt and operating costs, the second antibiotic bankruptcy in a year.
lesson: Multiple approved antibiotics still couldn't overcome the structurally poor economics of the antibacterial market.

name: Metromile
summary: Pay-per-mile auto insurance startup that went public via SPAC in 2021 and was acquired by Lemonade in an all-stock deal in 2022 at a steep discount to its earlier valuation, after failing to reach profitable scale.
lesson: A distressed-sale acquisition can be the realistic best outcome once a capital-intensive insurtech's growth-at-a-loss model runs out of runway.

name: Miasolé
summary: Thin-film solar panel maker that was sold at a steep discount to a Chinese conglomerate after burning through hundreds of millions without reaching profitability.
lesson: Being among the most heavily funded startups in a sector is no guarantee against a distressed fire-sale exit.

name: Midway Games
summary: The Mortal Kombat publisher accumulated heavy debt and underperforming titles, filing Chapter 11 and selling assets piecemeal.
lesson: Carrying too much corporate debt while release quality declines is a slow-motion bankruptcy.

name: Mint
summary: Pioneering personal finance and budgeting app, acquired by Intuit for $170M in 2009, was shut down in 2024 with users pushed to migrate to Credit Karma.
lesson: Even category-defining products get sunset when a parent company decides consolidation serves its strategy better.

name: Misfit
summary: Made stylish fitness trackers and was acquired by Fossil Group in 2015, but Fossil shut down the Misfit wearables brand in 2018.
lesson: A successful design-led exit doesn't guarantee the product survives inside a larger portfolio company.

name: Mixer
summary: Microsoft's live-streaming platform never closed the gap with Twitch despite exclusive deals with stars like Ninja, and was shut down in 2020.
lesson: Buying exclusive star creators can't overcome an entrenched platform's network effects and habit formation.

name: ModCloth
summary: Indie fashion ecommerce retailer sold to Walmart in 2017 for a fraction of its funding, then resold to Go Global Retail in 2019 before winding down meaningful operations.
lesson: Acquisition by a larger retailer isn't a rescue if the underlying economics never improve.

name: Modsy
summary: 3D interior design and furniture ecommerce startup shut down in 2022 after failing to convert design engagement into sustainable furniture sales margins.
lesson: Rich design experiences don't guarantee conversion rates high enough to cover furniture ecommerce costs.

name: Motif Investing
summary: Thematic investing platform that let users build baskets of stocks around ideas shut down and sold its technology and some assets to Charles Schwab.
lesson: Novel investing UX alone can't outrun zero-commission incumbents once trading fees go to zero industry-wide.

name: MoviePass
summary: A subscription service offering nearly unlimited movie theater tickets for a flat monthly fee; the company paid full price for every ticket redeemed, resulting in catastrophic losses.
lesson: A subscription price below your per-unit cost of goods sold is not a growth strategy, it's a countdown to insolvency.

name: Mt. Gox
summary: Once handling over 70% of all bitcoin transactions, the Tokyo exchange collapsed after admitting roughly 850,000 BTC had been lost or stolen through years of undetected hacking.
lesson: Exchanges holding customer assets need auditable, professional-grade security from day one.

name: Munchery
summary: On-demand prepared meal delivery startup shut down abruptly in 2019, leaving customers and vendors unpaid.
lesson: Food delivery logistics have thin margins that heavy funding cannot mask indefinitely.

name: Myspace
summary: The dominant social network of the mid-2000s lost its lead to Facebook after clumsy redesigns, ad clutter, and management turmoil under News Corp ownership.
lesson: Acquisition by a media conglomerate can smother the product agility that made a startup win.

name: Namely
summary: HR and payroll software for mid-sized companies raised over $260M before being sold to Vensure Employer Services after struggling to grow into its valuation.
lesson: Raising at aggressive valuations can force an eventual down-market exit if growth doesn't keep pace.

name: Nanosolar
summary: Thin-film CIGS solar panel maker, once one of the most hyped cleantech startups, that quietly shut down after years of production delays and missed targets.
lesson: Heavy pre-revenue hype and funding cannot substitute for solving hard manufacturing yield problems.

name: Napster
summary: The pioneering peer-to-peer music-sharing service was shut down by court injunction in 2001-2002 after a landmark RIAA copyright infringement lawsuit.
lesson: A product built on unresolved legal liability can be shut down overnight regardless of user love.

name: Nasty Gal
summary: Vintage-turned-fast-fashion ecommerce retailer filed for bankruptcy in 2016 and was acquired out of bankruptcy by Boohoo.
lesson: Rapid scaling of headcount and inventory without matching operational discipline can sink even a hot brand.

name: National CineMedia
summary: The largest cinema advertising network in the US filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in 2023 due to a heavy debt burden and slower-than-expected post-COVID theater attendance recovery.
lesson: Even a market-leading position can't offset a debt structure built for pre-pandemic attendance levels.

name: Nikola Corporation
summary: Hydrogen and electric truck maker went public via SPAC at an eye-popping valuation, was found by short-sellers to have faked a demo video, and filed for bankruptcy in 2025.
lesson: Fabricating product demonstrations to inflate a valuation is a criminal liability, not just a PR risk.

name: Northvolt
summary: Europe's flagship battery gigafactory startup filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy protection after production delays, quality issues, and a cash crunch despite billions raised.
lesson: Even massively funded gigafactory bets can fail if production ramp-up problems burn cash faster than customers can be delivered to.

name: Nuix
summary: The Australian e-discovery and forensic analytics company's 2020 IPO collapsed amid allegations of misleading investor forecasts, triggering a regulator investigation and executive departures.
lesson: Overpromising growth forecasts ahead of an IPO can trigger a governance crisis that outlasts the technology's actual value.

name: Nurx
summary: Direct-to-consumer telehealth startup for birth control and sexual health prescriptions was merged into Thirty Madison in 2022, with the standalone Nurx brand discontinued the following year after regulatory scrutiny over prescribing practices.
lesson: Consolidation is often the endpoint for DTC telehealth brands that can't sustain independent scale.

name: Oberlo
summary: Dropshipping tool that let Shopify merchants import products from AliExpress was acquired by Shopify in 2017 and discontinued in 2022 as Shopify shifted strategy toward other fulfillment partners.
lesson: Even category-defining tools can be discontinued once the parent platform's strategic priorities shift.

name: Ofo
summary: Chinese dockless bike-share giant that expanded to 20+ countries but collapsed under debt, deposit-refund scandals, and cutthroat competition with Mobike, retreating from most markets by 2019.
lesson: Blitzscaling into dozens of countries simultaneously without a path to profitability invites collapse.

name: Olive AI
summary: Healthcare AI automation startup once valued at $4B shut down and sold off business units after burning through nearly a billion dollars without delivering on its broad AI-automation promises.
lesson: Trying to automate too many disparate workflows at once can prevent an AI startup from proving value in any single one.

name: One Kings Lane
summary: Flash-sale home decor ecommerce site sold to Bed Bath & Beyond in a 2016 fire sale after raising over $200M, and its ecommerce operations were later wound down.
lesson: Flash-sale economics erode once novelty fades and customer acquisition costs rise.

name: Onerep
summary: Data-removal service that suffered a major reputational scandal in 2024 when it was revealed its founder had also created several of the people-search sites the service was meant to scrub users from.
lesson: A privacy service's credibility depends entirely on the integrity of its operators.

name: OneWeb
summary: LEO satellite broadband constellation builder filed Chapter 11 in March 2020 when a SoftBank-led funding round collapsed amid COVID-19 market turmoil; later rescued and merged into Eutelsat.
lesson: Capital-intensive satellite constellations need financing committed well before a downturn, not mid-buildout when investors can walk away.

name: OnLive
summary: A cloud-gaming pioneer years ahead of broadband infrastructure, OnLive burned through massive funding before selling its patents to Sony and shutting down.
lesson: Being technologically first doesn't matter if the underlying infrastructure isn't ready for mass adoption.

name: Outdoor Voices
summary: Athleisure D2C brand once valued near $400M was sold in a distressed deal in 2023 after years of executive turnover and cash problems.
lesson: Retail store overexpansion and leadership churn can unravel a hot D2C brand's momentum quickly.

name: Ouya
summary: The Android microconsole raised a then-record Kickstarter but shipped an underpowered device with a weak game library and was sold off to Razer in 2015.
lesson: Crowdfunding hype doesn't substitute for a compelling, differentiated hardware and software ecosystem.

name: Ozy Media
summary: The digital media startup collapsed within weeks in 2021 after a New York Times investigation and a fraudulent investor conference call exposed inflated traffic and revenue claims.
lesson: Fabricating metrics to investors is an existential risk, not a growth hack.

name: Pareteum Corporation
summary: Telecom connectivity software firm inflated revenue by over $40M from non-binding purchase orders; executives were later criminally charged and the company collapsed into Chapter 11.
lesson: Recognizing revenue on commitments customers never actually made is fraud, and it eventually destroys the company it was meant to prop up.

name: Parler
summary: The 'free speech' social network lost hosting and app-store access after the January 6, 2021 Capitol riot, and never recovered its user base.
lesson: Dependence on a handful of infrastructure and distribution gatekeepers is an existential single point of failure.

name: Parse
summary: Popular mobile backend-as-a-service acquired by Facebook for roughly $85M in 2013, then shut down in 2017 despite serving hundreds of thousands of apps, as it no longer fit Facebook's strategic priorities.
lesson: Even a widely-used developer platform can be shut down by an acquirer if it doesn't align with the parent company's core business strategy.

name: Participant Media
summary: A social-impact film and TV studio (Spotlight, An Inconvenient Truth) ceased operations in 2023 after nearly two decades and dozens of Oscar-nominated films.
lesson: A prestigious, mission-driven content strategy can still be financially unsustainable as distribution economics shift under streaming.

name: Path
summary: Intimate 'close friends' mobile social network never reached scale against Facebook; sold to Daum Kakao in 2015 and shut down in 2018.
lesson: A beautifully designed niche product can still lose to the incumbent's network effects.

name: Peapod
summary: One of the earliest online grocery delivery services, owned by Ahold Delhaize, was shut down in most U.S. markets in 2021 amid competition from Instacart and retailer-owned delivery.
lesson: First-mover advantage in ecommerce grocery erodes if the technology platform isn't continually modernized.

name: Pear Therapeutics
summary: Pioneer of FDA-cleared prescription digital therapeutics for addiction and insomnia went public via SPAC but filed for bankruptcy in 2023 after failing to secure adequate insurance reimbursement.
lesson: Prescription digital therapeutics need a proven reimbursement pathway before scaling burn.

name: Pebble
summary: Pioneering smartwatch that ran two record-breaking Kickstarter campaigns but couldn't compete with Apple Watch and Fitbit; assets acquired by Fitbit in 2016.
lesson: Crowdfunding hype doesn't guarantee a sustainable business against deep-pocketed competitors.

name: Peeple
summary: 'Yelp for people' app letting users publicly rate and review other individuals; faced immediate, overwhelming public backlash over privacy and harassment concerns and never gained real traction.
lesson: Applying a review-platform model directly to human beings crosses a trust threshold that most of the public will reject outright.

name: PeerStreet
summary: Real estate debt investing marketplace that let retail investors buy fractions of mortgage loans wound down operations after loan defaults spiked and liquidity dried up.
lesson: Marketplaces built on credit assets are extremely exposed to interest-rate and default cycles.

name: Peloton
summary: Connected fitness hardware maker whose pandemic-era boom collapsed post-2021, wiping out over 90% of its stock value and forcing out its founder amid mass layoffs.
lesson: Scaling manufacturing and headcount for pandemic-level demand becomes a crippling liability the moment demand normalizes.

name: Percolate
summary: Content marketing and brand management SaaS raised over $135M but was sold to Seismic in what was widely reported as a distressed, down-round acquisition.
lesson: Large enterprise contracts don't guarantee unit economics that support the cash burned to win them.

name: Petal
summary: Credit-card fintech using cash-flow underwriting to serve credit-invisible consumers; sold its card portfolio to Prosper in 2023 and wound down its core consumer card-issuing business after failing to reach profitability.
lesson: Alternative-underwriting credit products still require balance-sheet scale and cheap funding that early-stage fintechs struggle to secure.

name: Pets.com
summary: Online pet supplies retailer famous for its sock-puppet mascot burned through IPO proceeds on marketing and cheap shipping of heavy, low-margin goods before closing in 2000.
lesson: Marketing spend cannot compensate for a fundamentally unprofitable shipping and margin structure.

name: Pivotal Software
summary: Enterprise PaaS spin-off from EMC/VMware that IPO'd in 2018 but saw its stock collapse within a year, leading VMware to reacquire it in 2019 at a steep loss to public investors.
lesson: An enterprise PaaS built for a pre-Kubernetes world struggled once the market standardized on container orchestration.

name: Plastiq
summary: Bill-payment platform that let businesses pay non-card-accepting vendors with a credit card; filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in May 2023 after burning through venture funding without a durable margin.
lesson: Interchange-fee arbitrage business models are vulnerable when partner banks or card networks tighten terms.

name: Plenty
summary: SoftBank-backed indoor vertical farming company filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy after years of losses growing leafy greens indoors at high energy cost.
lesson: Massive venture funding can delay but not solve a fundamentally challenging cost structure in indoor agriculture.

name: Pluralsight
summary: Tech-skills learning platform that IPO'd in 2018 but saw its stock collapse amid slowing enterprise growth, leading Vista Equity Partners to take it private in 2021 at a steep valuation discount.
lesson: Enterprise subscription growth must keep pace with public-market expectations or valuations reset sharply.

name: PredPol / Geolitica
summary: Predictive policing software rebranded as Geolitica ceased operations at the end of 2023 after studies found it barely more accurate than random guessing and reinforced racial bias.
lesson: Predictive policing algorithms that reinforce existing patrol bias without proven crime-reduction results eventually lose government customers.

name: Project Ara (Google)
summary: Google's modular smartphone project let users swap out camera, battery, and other modules, but was cancelled in 2016 after failing to solve reliability and cost engineering challenges.
lesson: Modular hardware sounds compelling but real-world engineering tradeoffs can defeat the concept.

name: Prosper Marketplace
summary: One of the original P2P lending marketplaces that underwent a major crisis in 2016 with mass layoffs and investor pullback, and has operated at sharply reduced scale in the years since.
lesson: Marketplace lenders depend on institutional investor confidence in loan quality, which can evaporate industry-wide after a single peer's scandal.

name: Proterra
summary: Electric bus and battery maker that went public via SPAC in 2021 but filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in 2023 due to rising costs, supply chain issues, and cash burn, later selling its bus unit to Phoenix Motor.
lesson: Even a well-regarded electric bus product with real transit customers can be undone by manufacturing cost overruns.

name: Proteus Digital Health
summary: Pioneer of ingestible 'digital pill' sensors that tracked medication adherence failed to find commercial traction with payers and providers and filed for bankruptcy in 2020.
lesson: A technically impressive product still needs a clear reimbursement path to become a business.

name: Quartet Health
summary: Mental-health referral and care-coordination platform that connected primary care patients to behavioral health providers shut down operations in 2023 after failing to reach profitability.
lesson: Coordination platforms need a durable revenue model beyond thin per-referral fees.

name: Quibi
summary: Jeffrey Katzenberg and Meg Whitman's short-form premium streaming service raised $1.75B for mobile-only shows but shut down just six months after launch amid the pandemic and weak demand.
lesson: Massive funding and star power cannot substitute for validating that people actually want the format.

name: Quidsi (Diapers.com)
summary: Diapers.com and related sites were acquired by Amazon in 2011 for $545M after a fierce price war, then shut down entirely by Amazon in 2017.
lesson: Competing on price against Amazon in its own category is a losing long-term strategy even with an acquisition exit.

name: Quirky
summary: Crowdsourced invention platform that launched Wink smart-home devices; overexpanded into too many simultaneous product lines and filed for bankruptcy in 2015.
lesson: Spreading capital across dozens of unproven products at once starves any one of them of the resources to succeed.

name: Quixey
summary: Mobile app search engine raised over $200M including from SoftBank, but shut down amid allegations that executives had misrepresented revenue figures to investors.
lesson: Inflating metrics to secure large late-stage rounds eventually catches up with a company.

name: Rally Rd
summary: Fractional collectibles investing platform shut down its secondary trading marketplace after regulatory and liquidity challenges made the model unworkable.
lesson: Fractionalized alternative-asset marketplaces need real secondary liquidity, which is hard to bootstrap from scratch.

name: Range Fuels
summary: Cellulosic ethanol producer that shut its Georgia plant after failing to convert wood waste into fuel at commercial scale.
lesson: Lab-scale conversion chemistry often does not translate cleanly to industrial-scale biofuel production.

name: ReachNow
summary: BMW's US car-sharing and ride-hailing service launched in Seattle and other cities but was shut down in 2019 as BMW consolidated mobility efforts into Share Now.
lesson: Automaker mobility spinoffs often get folded or killed when parent-company strategy consolidates.

name: Redbox
summary: The DVD-kiosk giant tried to pivot into streaming as physical rental declined, was acquired by Chicken Soup for the Soul Entertainment in 2022, and filed for Chapter 7 bankruptcy in 2024.
lesson: A legacy hardware business rarely out-runs the decline of its core product just by bolting on a streaming app.

name: REEF Technology
summary: Parking-lot-turned-logistics-hub company (originally ParkJockey) that raised a huge SoftBank-led round at a $12.6B valuation but sold off major parking and delivery-kitchen units in 2023 amid deep losses.
lesson: Pivoting a parking-lot network into multiple unrelated logistics businesses simultaneously diluted focus and burned cash.

name: Relativity Media
summary: Ryan Kavanaugh's film and TV studio used complex financing structures to fund a rapid slate of movies, filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in 2015, and collapsed again in 2018 under unsustainable debt.
lesson: Financial engineering can fund rapid growth but doesn't fix an underlying business that loses money on its core product.

name: Rethink Robotics
summary: Maker of the Baxter and Sawyer collaborative industrial robots shut down after failing to find enough paying manufacturing customers.
lesson: Being technically groundbreaking in industrial robotics isn't sufficient if factory customers aren't yet ready to adopt at scale.

name: RethinkDB
summary: Open-source distributed document database that built a beloved developer community but could not convert usage into a sustainable business as MongoDB dominated the space.
lesson: Developer love and technical elegance don't guarantee a viable commercial model against an entrenched market leader.

name: Revolv
summary: Made a popular smart-home hub, was acquired by Google/Nest in 2014, then Nest bricked every customer's device in 2016 by shutting down the servers with no refunds.
lesson: Acquisition can be a death sentence for a hardware product if the acquirer has no roadmap for it.

name: RightScale
summary: Early cloud management platform leader that lost its market position to native multi-cloud tooling and was ultimately acquired by Flexera at a valuation far below its peak.
lesson: First-mover cloud management platforms lost relevance as AWS, Azure, and GCP built increasingly capable native management tools.

name: Rooster Teeth
summary: A pioneering internet content company with a fiercely loyal fan community was shut down in 2024 by Warner Bros. Discovery after 21 years amid broader corporate cost-cutting.
lesson: A profitable niche community-driven brand can still be cut when a parent conglomerate prioritizes company-wide cost reduction.

name: Root Insurance
summary: Usage-based car insurance startup that IPO'd in 2020 at a multi-billion-dollar valuation; its stock has since fallen more than 95% amid persistent underwriting losses and high customer acquisition costs.
lesson: Telematics-based pricing didn't solve insurtech's fundamental problem: acquiring and retaining customers profitably in a thin-margin, heavily regulated line of business.

name: Rosetta Stone
summary: Once-dominant CD-ROM/software language brand whose stock fell as free mobile apps like Duolingo disrupted its category; acquired by IXL Learning in 2021 at a steep discount.
lesson: A premium paid-software model is highly vulnerable to free, mobile-native disruptors targeting the same need.

name: Running Tide
summary: Ocean-based carbon removal startup backed by Shopify and Stripe that shut down after struggling to prove measurable, verifiable removal at scale.
lesson: Novel ocean carbon removal approaches face immense measurement and verification hurdles that can undercut buyer confidence.

name: Sangamo Therapeutics
summary: Pioneer of zinc-finger gene editing has seen a string of discontinued programs and multiple partner exits despite three decades of platform development.
lesson: An early technological lead in gene editing erodes quickly once CRISPR-based competitors demonstrate cleaner clinical data.

name: ScaleFactor
summary: 'AI-powered' small-business bookkeeping SaaS shut down after a Forbes investigation revealed its automation was largely manual human bookkeeping that frequently produced errors.
lesson: Marketing a product as AI-automated when it isn't reliably working can destroy trust once customers are exposed to the failures.

name: Secret
summary: Anonymous confession-sharing app raised heavily on a viral launch but was shut down by founders after becoming a magnet for bullying and harassment.
lesson: Founders should be willing to shut down a growing product if it can't be made safe.

name: SelectQuote
summary: Publicly traded insurance distribution platform lost over 90% of its market value after Medicare Advantage commission changes and customer acquisition cost inflation crushed its margins.
lesson: Insurance distribution businesses reliant on paid customer acquisition are highly exposed to commission rule changes.

name: Selina
summary: Latin America-born hostel/co-living hospitality brand expanded to 150+ locations, went public via SPAC, and filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy amid unsustainable lease obligations.
lesson: Rapid global real-estate expansion without proving profitability at existing locations first is a recurring hospitality failure pattern.

name: Shift Technologies
summary: Online used car buying and selling marketplace went public via SPAC in 2020 and wound down operations in 2023 after years of heavy losses.
lesson: Online used-car marketplaces face brutal working-capital and reconditioning costs that scale with volume.

name: ShopClues
summary: Indian online marketplace for unbranded and discounted goods, once valued over $1B, was sold in a distressed 2019 deal for a small fraction of capital raised.
lesson: Marketplaces built around counterfeit-prone, low-trust categories struggle to sustain premium valuations.

name: ShotSpotter (SoundThinking)
summary: Gunshot-detection technology used by police departments has faced a wave of city contract cancellations over accuracy and bias concerns.
lesson: Public-safety AI tools face an existential risk when independent audits and community backlash undermine trust faster than sales growth.

name: Shuddle
summary: 'Uber for kids' ride-hailing startup providing vetted drivers for unaccompanied minors that shut down in 2016 after failing to raise a follow-on funding round.
lesson: Trust-and-safety-heavy niches like child transportation require expensive vetting infrastructure that's hard to fund at startup speed.

name: Shyp
summary: On-demand package pickup and shipping startup shut down in 2018 after failing to find a scalable, profitable model.
lesson: Convenience-as-a-service businesses need pricing that actually covers labor and logistics costs.

name: Sidecar
summary: Early ride-hailing and courier pioneer that introduced features later copied by Uber and Lyft but shut down its ride service in 2015, selling assets to General Motors.
lesson: Being first with an innovative feature doesn't matter if better-funded competitors can copy it and outspend you.

name: Sidewalk Labs
summary: Alphabet's smart-city venture cancelled its flagship Quayside waterfront redevelopment project in Toronto in 2020 amid privacy backlash and pandemic-driven economic uncertainty.
lesson: Smart-city projects that centralize resident data collection invite intense privacy and governance scrutiny that can kill a project outright.

name: Signature Bank
summary: New York bank with a large crypto and digital-asset deposit base that was closed by regulators in March 2023 amid contagion from Silicon Valley Bank's collapse and a rapid depositor run.
lesson: Uninsured deposit concentration can turn a solvent-on-paper bank into a forced closure within days once confidence breaks.

name: Silicon Valley Bank
summary: Bank central to the startup ecosystem that failed in March 2023 after rising rates devalued its bond portfolio and a concentrated, tech-sector depositor base fled within 48 hours.
lesson: A depositor base concentrated in one interconnected industry can coordinate a bank run faster than traditional liquidity risk models assume.

name: Silvercar
summary: Premium airport rental-car startup (all-silver Audi A4 fleet) that Audi acquired in 2017 for an estimated $300M+, then shut down entirely in 2019 as Audi refocused its mobility strategy.
lesson: A lucrative acquisition exit doesn't guarantee long-term survival once the acquirer's strategic priorities change.

name: Silvergate Bank
summary: California bank that pivoted to become the primary banking rail for crypto exchanges; collapsed in March 2023 after FTX's failure triggered a deposit run it could not cover without fire-selling securities.
lesson: Concentrating deposits in one volatile client sector creates correlated withdrawal risk that ordinary liquidity buffers cannot absorb.

name: Simple
summary: One of the earliest US neobanks, acquired by BBVA in 2014 for $117M; shut down in 2021 after PNC acquired BBVA USA and decided to consolidate customers onto its own digital platform.
lesson: Being an acquisition pioneer doesn't guarantee survival once the acquirer itself gets acquired.

name: Skip Scooters
summary: San Francisco-based e-scooter operator that lost several city permits and was later acquired by Helbiz, which itself unwound the business.
lesson: Permit-dependent micromobility businesses live and die by city regulatory relationships.

name: Sky Mavis (Axie Infinity)
summary: The play-to-earn game's Ronin sidechain bridge was hacked for $625M in 2022, and player and economic activity collapsed as crypto gaming interest cooled.
lesson: A play-to-earn economy is only as strong as the security of its underlying blockchain infrastructure.

name: Slync
summary: Freight and supply-chain orchestration software startup that shut down in 2023 after its founder and CEO were criminally charged with defrauding investors.
lesson: Founder fraud can vaporize a well-funded logistics-tech company overnight, regardless of the underlying product.

name: SmileDirectClub
summary: Direct-to-consumer clear-aligner company that bypassed in-person orthodontists faced lawsuits over safety and regulatory pushback from dentists, and shut down entirely in December 2023 after bankruptcy.
lesson: Disintermediating licensed clinicians invites regulatory and legal resistance that can outlast the growth story.

name: Snapdeal
summary: Indian ecommerce marketplace once valued near $6.5B saw a collapsed merger attempt with Flipkart in 2017, followed by mass layoffs and a drastic valuation markdown, and now operates as a much smaller value-fashion platform.
lesson: Overexpanding a marketplace ahead of proven unit economics leaves no cushion when a funding-driven price war arrives.

name: Solyndra
summary: Solar panel maker that received a $535M DOE loan guarantee but collapsed when Chinese manufacturers crashed silicon and panel prices.
lesson: A capital-intensive hardware bet on a specific manufacturing process can be wiped out overnight by commodity price collapse.

name: Sonder Holdings
summary: Tech-enabled short-term rental operator went public via SPAC, saw a proposed Marriott partnership collapse, and wound down operations in 2025.
lesson: Leasing large real-estate portfolios to run a hospitality brand requires far deeper capital reserves than software-style startups assume.

name: SourceForge
summary: Once the dominant open-source code hosting site, SourceForge's reputation collapsed after it began bundling adware with installers in 2015, driving nearly all projects to migrate to GitHub.
lesson: Monetization decisions that betray developer trust can destroy a platform's community faster than any competitor.

name: Spin
summary: E-scooter startup acquired by Ford in 2018, then sold to Tier Mobility in 2022 after Ford failed to make micromobility work within its business.
lesson: Corporate-owned mobility units often get deprioritized and divested when parent strategy shifts.

name: Spirit Airlines
summary: US ultra-low-cost carrier filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy after a proposed JetBlue merger was blocked by regulators and it couldn't return to profitability post-COVID.
lesson: A blocked exit can leave a structurally unprofitable business with no path forward but bankruptcy.

name: SpoonRocket
summary: Instant meal delivery startup promising 10-minute delivery shut down in 2016 after running out of funding.
lesson: Extreme delivery-speed promises multiply operating costs beyond what thin food margins can support.

name: Sprig
summary: On-demand healthy meal delivery service shut down in 2017 after failing to make its kitchen-to-door model profitable.
lesson: Vertically integrated food delivery requires enormous capital to reach the density needed for profitability.

name: Spring (Shop Spring)
summary: Mobile shopping marketplace app aggregating brands into one checkout experience shut down in 2017 after failing to gain enough traction against larger platforms.
lesson: Aggregator marketplaces need a real distribution edge, not just a better checkout UX.

name: Stability AI
summary: Open-source generative AI company (Stable Diffusion) faced severe cash-flow problems, unpaid compute bills, and a wave of researcher departures, culminating in CEO Emad Mostaque's resignation in 2024.
lesson: Open-source AI model releases build brand but not necessarily a monetization engine to fund the compute costs behind them.

name: Starsky Robotics
summary: Autonomous trucking company aimed at long-haul freight supply chains shut down in 2020 after failing to raise a new funding round.
lesson: Autonomous freight technology took far longer to commercialize than early investor timelines assumed.

name: Stitch Labs
summary: Inventory and order management software for ecommerce sellers was acquired by Square in 2019 and discontinued in 2020 as Square consolidated its commerce tools.
lesson: Being acquired doesn't guarantee product continuity if the acquirer's roadmap diverges.

name: Stockpile
summary: Gift-card-based stock investing platform aimed at giving fractional shares as presents shut down after failing to convert gift redemptions into a durable trading business.
lesson: A clever acquisition gimmick doesn't necessarily build a habitual investing product.

name: STX Entertainment
summary: A film and TV studio that raised hundreds of millions, went public via SPAC in 2020, saw its stock collapse, and was ultimately absorbed into Lionsgate in a distressed 2023 deal.
lesson: A SPAC listing can provide capital and a public valuation but doesn't fix a studio's underlying content economics.

name: Summon
summary: Los Angeles-based ride-hailing startup that attempted to compete directly with Uber and Lyft in a single metro market and shut down within two years.
lesson: Regional ride-hailing challengers rarely survive head-on competition against nationally-scaled incumbents.

name: Sun Basket
summary: Organic and specialty-diet meal kit subscription service underwent massive layoffs and downsizing starting in 2022 as investor funding for meal kits dried up.
lesson: Premium positioning doesn't exempt meal-kit subscriptions from the category's structural retention and logistics costs.

name: SunEdison
summary: Once the world's largest renewable energy developer, SunEdison filed for bankruptcy after an aggressive debt-fueled acquisition spree collapsed under its own weight.
lesson: Scaling a renewables developer through aggressive leverage and acquisitions can outrun the cash flow needed to service that debt.

name: Sunnova Energy
summary: Residential solar and storage financing company that filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in 2025 after high interest rates and reduced federal incentives crushed its leveraged leasing model.
lesson: A debt-heavy leasing model for residential solar is highly exposed to rising interest rates and policy shifts.

name: SunPower
summary: Longtime residential solar installer and panel maker that filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in 2024 after liquidity problems and a collapsing dealer network.
lesson: Decades of market presence don't protect against a liquidity crisis when financing costs rise and demand softens.

name: Superpedestrian
summary: MIT-spinout maker of the LINK scooter with proprietary safety tech that shut down its micromobility operations in late 2023 after failing to raise further funding.
lesson: Even differentiated hardware-safety technology couldn't overcome the capital intensity of scooter-fleet operations.

name: Sycamore Networks
summary: Optical networking equipment maker was one of the dot-com era's most extreme boom-bust stories, falling from a peak market cap near $45B to near zero before winding down.
lesson: Valuations built on the assumption that internet bandwidth demand would grow indefinitely can evaporate the moment the buildout cycle pauses.

name: Synapse Financial Technologies
summary: Banking-as-a-service middleware provider that collapsed into bankruptcy in 2024, leaving reconciliation discrepancies between partner banks and fintech apps that froze an estimated $85M+ in end-customer deposits.
lesson: In layered banking-as-a-service stacks, poor ledger reconciliation between middleware and partner banks can strand real customer money.

name: TAL Education Group
summary: Publicly traded Chinese tutoring giant's stock collapsed over 90% after Beijing banned for-profit tutoring in core school subjects.
lesson: A single sweeping regulatory policy change can instantly dismantle an entire national industry segment.

name: Telltale Games
summary: The episodic-adventure pioneer overextended into too many simultaneous licensed titles and collapsed almost overnight, laying off 250 staff with little warning.
lesson: Chasing every available IP license can outrun a studio's production capacity and cash runway.

name: Terraform Labs
summary: Creator of the TerraUSD algorithmic stablecoin and LUNA token; the ecosystem collapsed in May 2022 when UST lost its dollar peg, erasing roughly $40B in value within days.
lesson: An algorithmic stablecoin backed only by a sister token's market confidence has no real collateral floor.

name: Terraform Labs (Terra/Luna)
summary: Creator of the algorithmic stablecoin UST and sister token LUNA, which de-pegged and collapsed in May 2022, wiping out roughly $40B in value; the company filed for bankruptcy in 2024 and its founder was later found liable for fraud.
lesson: Algorithmic stablecoins backed by a reflexive token rather than real reserves are structurally vulnerable to bank-run dynamics.

name: The Iron Yard
summary: One of the largest US coding bootcamp networks with 15+ campuses shut down abruptly after parent Apollo Education Group failed to find a buyer.
lesson: Fast multi-city expansion outpaced sustainable unit economics for coding bootcamps.

name: The League
summary: Elite matchmaking app for ambitious professionals; acquired by Match Group in 2019 and shut down in 2024 after failing to scale beyond a niche audience.
lesson: An exclusivity-based niche can build buzz but rarely reaches the scale needed to survive inside a portfolio of larger competitors.

name: The Yes
summary: AI-powered personalized fashion shopping app was acquired by Pinterest in 2022 for its technology and talent, then shut down in 2023.
lesson: An acqui-hire exit for the team doesn't mean the product itself found lasting product-market fit.

name: Theranos
summary: Blood-testing startup claimed its Edison device could run hundreds of tests from a finger-prick, but the technology never worked and results were faked or run on standard lab equipment.
lesson: Regulatory and clinical claims cannot be faked indefinitely once real patients and inspectors are involved.

name: Therium Capital Management
summary: One of the UK's largest litigation funders entered administration in 2023 after running into severe liquidity problems funding a portfolio of long-duration lawsuits.
lesson: Litigation funding ties up capital for years with uncertain payout timing, which can crush a fund's liquidity even with a strong case portfolio.

name: Thomas Cook
summary: Britain's oldest travel agent collapsed into compulsory liquidation in 2019 under £1.7B of debt, triggering the largest peacetime repatriation in UK history.
lesson: Legacy travel giants weighed down by debt and physical infrastructure can be wiped out fast by shifting booking habits.

name: THQ
summary: The publisher overspent on flops like the uDraw tablet and uneven franchises, burning through cash reserves until bankruptcy and a 2012 asset liquidation.
lesson: Diversifying into unproven hardware bets can sink an otherwise healthy publisher.

name: Three Arrows Capital
summary: Singapore-based crypto hedge fund ordered into liquidation after over-leveraged bets, including a large Terra/LUNA position, wiped out its capital.
lesson: Leverage on illiquid, correlated crypto bets turns a single bad trade into systemic contagion.

name: Tier Mobility
summary: European scooter and e-bike sharing leader that acquired Spin in 2022 but filed for insolvency proceedings in 2024 after failing to reach profitability at scale.
lesson: Market leadership in micromobility doesn't guarantee profitability when vehicle replacement costs stay high.

name: Totsy
summary: Flash-sale ecommerce site for baby and kids products shut down in 2013 as the flash-sale category became oversaturated.
lesson: Entering a flash-sale niche late, after category leaders are entrenched, leaves little room to build differentiation.

name: Tradeshift
summary: B2B procurement and supply-chain platform once valued near $2.7B ran into severe financial distress, restructuring and selling off assets after years of heavy losses.
lesson: Raising enormous sums for a broad procurement platform vision without matching revenue can lead to a slow-motion collapse even at unicorn scale.

name: Travis CI
summary: Pioneering hosted CI service for open-source and GitHub projects that was acquired by Idera in 2019, cut most of its team in 2021, and declined into irrelevance as GitHub Actions took over.
lesson: First-mover advantage in developer tooling erodes quickly once the platform you depend on launches a competing native feature.

name: Trion Worlds
summary: The Rift and Defiance MMO studio never replicated early success at scale and was wound down, with remaining assets sold off piecemeal.
lesson: MMO development costs and live-ops overhead are brutal if a title doesn't retain a large enough subscriber base.

name: Trov
summary: On-demand, single-item insurance app that let users toggle coverage for gadgets and gear shut down after failing to reach profitability and pivoting to a B2B licensing model that also stalled.
lesson: Micro-insurance for niche items struggled to generate enough premium volume to cover acquisition and claims costs.

name: Trunk Club
summary: Personal styling and menswear ecommerce service was acquired by Nordstrom for $350M in 2014 but was shut down in 2022 after years of losses.
lesson: High-touch personal styling has cost structures that are hard to scale profitably even under a retail parent.

name: Trustify
summary: A marketplace connecting clients with private investigators for legal investigations collapsed amid allegations that its CEO defrauded investors and misused company funds.
lesson: Founder integrity failures can destroy an otherwise viable marketplace business overnight.

name: Tumblr
summary: Once bought by Yahoo for $1.1 billion, the blogging platform was mismanaged under Yahoo/Verizon ownership and a 2018 adult-content ban drove users away, ending in a sale to Automattic for ~$3M.
lesson: A large acquisition price can be destroyed by years of neglect and a single poorly executed content policy change.

name: TuSimple
summary: Autonomous trucking company that IPO'd in 2021 but wound down its US trucking operations in 2024 amid a federal fraud investigation.
lesson: Governance scandals can destroy an AV company's public-market credibility even after a successful IPO.

name: uBiome
summary: Microbiome-testing startup was raided by the FBI over billing practices for its gut-health tests and filed for bankruptcy months later amid fraud allegations against its founders.
lesson: Aggressive insurance billing practices in consumer diagnostics invite regulatory scrutiny that can end a company overnight.

name: Udacity
summary: Nanodegree pioneer once valued above $1B that struggled with completion rates and enterprise competition, leading to a majority stake sale to Accenture in 2023 at a steep discount.
lesson: Repeated pivots between consumer MOOCs and enterprise Nanodegrees never found a durable, scalable model.

name: University of Phoenix
summary: Once the largest US for-profit university, enrollment collapsed from roughly 470,000 to under 100,000 students; parent Apollo Education paid a $191M FTC settlement in 2019 over deceptive advertising.
lesson: Scale built on aggressive marketing claims is unsustainable once regulators and public scrutiny catch up.

name: Untether AI
summary: Toronto-based startup built energy-efficient at-memory AI inference chips but failed to pivot fast enough to the generative-AI hardware boom.
lesson: Being early to an efficient-compute thesis doesn't matter if you can't ship product before the market's needs shift underneath you.

name: Vacasa
summary: Largest US vacation rental property manager went public via SPAC, lost over 95% of its stock value amid heavy losses, and was taken private by Casago.
lesson: Rapid roll-up growth in property management can mask deteriorating per-unit profitability until public markets expose it.

name: Value America
summary: Early online superstore that IPO'd in 1999 collapsed in 2000 amid fulfillment failures and executive turmoil.
lesson: Ecommerce breadth without reliable fulfillment infrastructure destroys customer trust fast.

name: Velo3D
summary: Metal additive-manufacturing systems maker went public via SPAC in 2021 and filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in 2025 after persistent losses and customer concentration in aerospace.
lesson: Metal 3D printing requires enormous capital for R&D and service, and revenue concentration in a few large customers is fragile.

name: Vessel
summary: A premium early-access video subscription service for YouTube creators struggled to gain traction and was acquired and dissolved by Verizon within two years.
lesson: Paying creators for exclusivity windows is hard to sustain when the free version is one tab away.

name: Vice Media
summary: Once valued near $6B, the youth media empire filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in 2023 after years of missed revenue targets, mass layoffs, and an unsustainable ad-and-studio model.
lesson: A soaring private valuation is not a substitute for a profitable core business.

name: Vine
summary: Short-form looping video pioneer acquired by Twitter before launch; failed to build creator monetization and was shut down in 2016-17 as Instagram and Snapchat surged.
lesson: Owning a creative format isn't enough if you never build a way for creators to earn on it.

name: VIPKid
summary: Once a $3B+ unicorn connecting North American tutors with Chinese children for English lessons, gutted almost overnight by China's 2021 ban on for-profit online tutoring.
lesson: A single-market regulatory dependency can destroy a business overnight regardless of prior scale.

name: Virgin Hyperloop
summary: Richard Branson-backed hyperloop startup that ran the first passenger hyperloop test in 2020 but pivoted away from passenger transport and laid off half its staff in 2022, effectively ending its original mission.
lesson: Novel transportation modes face such steep regulatory and infrastructure hurdles that even successful tech demos don't guarantee commercialization.

name: Virgin Orbit
summary: Richard Branson's air-launched small-satellite rocket company filed for bankruptcy after a high-profile UK launch failure and inability to raise further funding.
lesson: A single high-visibility launch failure can freeze funding for a capital-intensive space startup with thin cash reserves.

name: Voatz
summary: Blockchain-based mobile voting app used in West Virginia elections, was abandoned by the state after MIT researchers found serious security vulnerabilities.
lesson: Mobile voting technology carries such high stakes that a single credible security audit can end government adoption overnight.

name: Vogo
summary: Bangalore-based dockless scooter rental startup backed by Ola that wound down operations in 2022 as parent Ola restructured its mobility investments.
lesson: A startup dependent on a single strategic investor is vulnerable to that investor's own portfolio triage decisions.

name: Volt Bank
summary: Australia's first licensed neobank, which returned customer deposits and handed back its banking license in mid-2022 after a planned $100M capital raise fell through.
lesson: Regulatory capital requirements mean a neobank's survival can hinge entirely on one funding round closing on time.

name: Volta Industries
summary: EV charging network known for ad-supported free charging stalls that filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy after failing to reach profitability post-SPAC merger.
lesson: An advertising-subsidized business model for capital-intensive charging infrastructure struggled to cover real hardware and site costs.

name: Volta Trucks
summary: Swedish electric commercial truck maker that filed for insolvency in late 2023 after its battery supplier Proterra went bankrupt, disrupting Volta's own production ramp.
lesson: Supply-chain dependency risk cascades: one supplier's bankruptcy can trigger a customer's collapse too.

name: Voyage
summary: Self-driving taxi startup focused on retirement communities that was acquired by Cruise in 2021, ending its run as an independent AV company.
lesson: Niche AV deployment strategies (like retirement communities) can prove a concept but rarely generate independent scale.

name: Voyager Digital
summary: Crypto brokerage and lender filed for Chapter 11 after Three Arrows Capital defaulted on a $650M loan, leaving a large hole in its balance sheet.
lesson: Concentrated counterparty exposure to a single hedge fund can sink an otherwise-operating lending business overnight.

name: Vroom
summary: Online used car retailer IPO'd in 2020 at a multi-billion dollar valuation but wound down its ecommerce car-buying operations in 2022 after mounting losses, pivoting to a finance-only company.
lesson: Even well-funded, high-profile IPOs can't outrun negative unit economics in capital-intensive categories.

name: Wanelo
summary: Social shopping app that let users browse and save products from online stores shut down in 2019 after losing relevance to Instagram and Pinterest shopping features.
lesson: Standalone social-shopping apps struggle once platform giants add native shopping features.

name: Weaveworks
summary: Pioneer of the GitOps methodology and Flux CD that ran out of funding and shut down in 2024 despite having defined an entire category of Kubernetes deployment tooling.
lesson: Defining a category doesn't guarantee capturing its commercial value if your own open-source project becomes a standard others monetize instead.

name: Webvan
summary: Dot-com era online grocery delivery company built massive automated warehouses ahead of demand and filed for bankruptcy in 2001, becoming a canonical dot-com bust.
lesson: Building expensive infrastructure years ahead of proven consumer demand is a fatal capital allocation mistake.

name: Wefox
summary: Once Europe's most valuable insurtech, the German digital insurance broker and carrier saw its valuation collapse, sold off units, and underwent emergency rescue financing amid governance and cash concerns.
lesson: Rapid multi-country insurance expansion funded by mega-rounds can unravel quickly once growth-at-all-costs financing dries up.

name: Weinstein Company
summary: A major independent film and TV studio co-founded by Harvey and Bob Weinstein; it collapsed into bankruptcy within months of the New York Times investigation exposing Harvey Weinstein's sexual assault and harassment.
lesson: Founder misconduct can destroy an entire company's value virtually overnight, regardless of its content library or track record.

name: WeWork
summary: Coworking company that once reached a $47B private valuation filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy after years of unsustainable long-term lease liabilities and a failed 2019 IPO.
lesson: Locking in long-term lease liabilities while selling short-term memberships creates a fatal duration mismatch in a downturn.

name: Wheels
summary: Seated e-moped/scooter startup that was acquired by Helbiz in 2022 as the broader micromobility sector consolidated amid financing pressure.
lesson: An oversaturated scooter market forced consolidation among undifferentiated players.

name: Winc
summary: Wine subscription and D2C ecommerce company (formerly Club W) went public via SPAC and filed for bankruptcy in 2024 amid falling subscriber counts.
lesson: Subscription commerce for discretionary categories is vulnerable to churn once novelty fades.

name: Wirecard
summary: German payment processor that fabricated €1.9B in nonexistent cash balances; collapsed into insolvency in 2020 after auditors and journalists exposed the fraud.
lesson: Audited financials and regulatory sign-off are not proof of legitimacy when a company controls its own auditors and regulators.

name: WiseBanyan
summary: Free robo-advisor marketed as 'the world's first free financial advisor' was acquired by Axos Bank and shut down as a standalone service.
lesson: A completely free robo-advisory product has no path to profitability without a monetizable add-on.

name: Wish (ContextLogic)
summary: Discount marketplace app connecting shoppers to Chinese sellers IPO'd in 2020 at a ~$14B valuation, then collapsed as user growth reversed and was sold to Qoo10 in 2024 for about $173M.
lesson: A marketplace built on cheap, slow-shipped goods struggles to retain users once competitors offer better experiences.

name: Wonga
summary: UK payday lender once valued over £1B that collapsed into administration in August 2018, overwhelmed by a wave of customer compensation claims following a regulatory crackdown on payday lending.
lesson: A high-margin lending model built on regulatory gaps collapses once those gaps close and historical customers can claim redress retroactively.

name: WOW air
summary: Icelandic ultra-low-cost long-haul carrier grew rapidly on cheap transatlantic fares but ran out of cash and ceased operations.
lesson: Ultra-low fares built on unsustainable unit economics collapse the moment fuel costs or funding tighten.

name: Wrisk
summary: UK insurtech offering personalized, app-based flexible insurance policies entered administration after running out of runway during a difficult fundraising climate.
lesson: Capital-intensive insurance underwriting models are especially vulnerable when venture funding tightens.

name: Xinja
summary: Australian neobank that launched with a licensed savings account offering a high interest rate; surrendered its banking license and returned deposits in December 2020 after failing to raise enough capital to sustain operations.
lesson: A neobank funding growth through an unsustainably high deposit rate needs a capital raise lined up well before the cash runs out.

name: Yieldstreet
summary: Alternative asset investing platform for retail investors faced SEC charges and an $8M settlement plus major loan defaults in its marine and other structured deals, damaging investor trust.
lesson: Alt-asset marketplaces carry underwriting risk that can boomerang onto the platform's reputation, not just the borrower.

name: Yik Yak
summary: Anonymous campus messaging app grew explosively then collapsed after anti-bullying backlash and a pivot to real usernames alienated its core users.
lesson: Anonymity that fuels viral growth can also fuel the abuse that kills the product.

name: Zagster
summary: Early bike-share operator serving campuses and cities that sold its assets to Superpedestrian's Spin brand in 2019 after failing to compete with dockless scooter rivals.
lesson: Station-based bike-share models struggled to compete once dockless scooters offered more convenience.

name: Zenefits
summary: HR and insurance-brokerage software company grew explosively then was engulfed by a licensing-compliance scandal in 2016, leading to leadership ousters and a diminished eventual wind-down.
lesson: Growth-at-all-costs culture in a regulated industry can create existential compliance risk.

name: Zillow Offers
summary: Zillow's iBuying division was shut down in November 2021 after the company's pricing algorithm systematically overpaid for homes, leading to roughly $881M in write-downs.
lesson: An algorithmic pricing model needs continuous recalibration against a fast-moving market or it will overpay at scale.

name: Zirx
summary: On-demand valet parking startup backed by Trinity Ventures and GV that shut down operations in 2017 after struggling to find a profitable business model.
lesson: Two well-funded valet-parking startups (Luxe and Zirx) failing in the same year signaled the category itself was structurally unprofitable.

name: Zulily
summary: Flash-sale ecommerce site for moms and kids IPO'd in 2015, was acquired by QVC parent Qurate for $2.4B, and shut down entirely in 2023.
lesson: A large acquisition doesn't guarantee survival if the core business model keeps losing ground to Amazon and social commerce.

name: Zume Pizza
summary: Robotic pizza-making delivery truck startup that raised $375M from SoftBank at a peak valuation near $4B, pivoted to sustainable packaging, then wound down entirely.
lesson: Flashy robotics can't substitute for validated product-market fit before scaling aggressively.

name: Zymergen
summary: AI/ML-driven synthetic biology company IPO'd but disclosed manufacturing and product failures shortly after, causing a stock collapse; was acquired by Ginkgo Bioworks at a fraction of its value.
lesson: AI-driven R&D claims need to be validated by real product yields before scaling go-to-market spend.

name: 1Password
summary: Password manager that stayed profitable and bootstrapped for over a decade before raising its first outside funding in 2019, later reaching a valuation of roughly $6.8 billion.
lesson: A profitable, product-led company can wait over a decade to raise venture capital, entering from a position of strength rather than need.

name: A24
summary: An independent studio built a reputation for distinctive, critically acclaimed films and expanded into television and a strong consumer merchandise brand.
lesson: A sharply defined creative identity can become a durable brand and business moat in a commoditized film industry.

name: Adyen
summary: Dutch payments platform serving large global enterprises like Uber, Spotify, and Netflix through a single unified processing stack; one of Europe's most profitable fintech IPOs.
lesson: Focusing on large enterprise merchants with complex multi-country needs can command higher margins than mass-market SMB payments.

name: Affirm
summary: Buy-now-pay-later lender founded by Max Levchin that emphasizes transparent, interest-disclosed installment loans and has built deep retail partnerships including Amazon and Shopify.
lesson: Differentiating a BNPL product on transparent pricing (no late fees, clear APR) built merchant and consumer trust that pure convenience-focused rivals lacked.

name: Airbnb
summary: Home-sharing marketplace grew from air-mattress rentals into the world's largest hospitality platform, surviving a COVID-19 near-death experience to go public in 2020.
lesson: Deep two-sided network effects and brand trust let a marketplace recover even from a near-total demand collapse.

name: Airwallex
summary: Australian-founded cross-border payments and banking infrastructure platform serving businesses globally; reached unicorn status with strong growth in Asia-Pacific, North America, and Europe.
lesson: Building a global multi-currency payments network from an Asia-Pacific base gave Airwallex an edge in cross-border corridors US-centric competitors underserved.

name: Alnylam Pharmaceuticals
summary: Pioneered RNA interference as a drug modality, winning FDA approval for the first-ever RNAi therapeutic in 2018, followed by several more approved RNAi drugs.
lesson: Persisting through nearly two decades of platform validation risk can create an entirely new drug modality and category leadership.

name: Ampere Computing
summary: Cloud-native Arm server processor designer, backed by Carlyle and Oracle, was acquired by SoftBank in a $6.5 billion all-cash deal that completed in late 2025.
lesson: Focusing purely on power-efficient cloud server chips built enough enterprise credibility to command a multi-billion-dollar exit.

name: Anduril
summary: Defense hardware and autonomous systems company founded by Palmer Luckey rapidly scaled to a $30B+ valuation by selling AI-powered systems directly to militaries.
lesson: Building hardware specifically for a well-funded, motivated buyer with urgent needs can scale revenue exceptionally fast.

name: Anker
summary: Started selling replacement laptop batteries on Amazon, then expanded into chargers, power banks, and a wide consumer electronics portfolio.
lesson: Starting narrow on a marketplace with a commodity category and reinvesting profits can build a global consumer electronics conglomerate without heavy VC funding.

name: Anthropic
summary: AI safety-focused research company founded by former OpenAI researchers developed the Claude family of models and grew rapidly into one of the leading frontier AI labs.
lesson: A clear safety-and-research-driven mission combined with strong technical execution can attract major strategic capital quickly.

name: Astera Labs
summary: Data-center connectivity chip maker IPO'd on Nasdaq in March 2024, popping over 70% on debut as AI infrastructure demand for its interconnect chips surged.
lesson: Riding the AI infrastructure buildout as a picks-and-shovels connectivity supplier let Astera capture value without competing directly with Nvidia.

name: Atlassian
summary: Australian enterprise software company grew profitably for over a decade without a traditional sales team before its 2015 IPO.
lesson: A self-serve, product-led growth motion can out-scale traditional enterprise sales.

name: Augury
summary: AI-based predictive maintenance company using acoustic and vibration sensors reached unicorn status serving major manufacturers like PepsiCo and Colgate-Palmolive.
lesson: Machine-health monitoring with clear, quantifiable downtime-avoidance ROI drives strong enterprise adoption.

name: Aurora Innovation
summary: Autonomous trucking company founded by ex-Google, Tesla and Uber ATG leaders that acquired Uber's self-driving unit in 2020, went public via SPAC in 2021, and launched driverless commercial freight hauls in Texas in 2024 while rivals TuSimple and Embark collapsed.
lesson: Surviving the AV trucking shakeout required both deep technical credibility and enough capital discipline to outlast a wave of competitor bankruptcies.

name: Babbel
summary: German language-learning app that built a profitable subscription business well before rivals, staying private and cash-flow positive since 2016.
lesson: A disciplined subscription model can beat venture-fueled growth in language learning.

name: Badoo
summary: Large international social-discovery dating app founded by Andrey Andreev that became the anchor asset of Bumble Inc., which went public in 2021.
lesson: Global-scale network effects built over many years can become the foundation for a much larger public company.

name: Beats by Dre
summary: Built a premium headphone brand around celebrity co-founders and pop-culture marketing, becoming Apple's largest acquisition at the time.
lesson: Brand and cultural marketing can differentiate consumer audio hardware even when the underlying components aren't class-leading.

name: Betterment
summary: Pioneer robo-advisor that has continued to grow assets under management and expanded into cash management, retirement, and advisor-focused products, remaining one of the largest independent robo-advisors.
lesson: Automated low-fee investing found durable demand among younger investors underserved by traditional human advisors.

name: BetterUp
summary: Enterprise coaching and professional-development platform that reached unicorn status with a $4.7B valuation in 2021.
lesson: Packaging human coaching as a scalable enterprise benefit created a large new category.

name: Billie
summary: D2C women's razor and body care brand built a strong brand identity around body positivity and was acquired by Edgewell Personal Care in 2021 for $310M after an earlier P&G deal was blocked by the FTC.
lesson: A distinctive brand positioning can command a premium acquisition price even in a crowded razor category.

name: Binance
summary: Grew from a $15M ICO into the world's largest crypto exchange by volume; despite a 2023 DOJ settlement including a $4.3B fine, the exchange continued dominating global volume.
lesson: Aggressive early global expansion and product breadth can build network effects large enough to survive severe regulatory penalties.

name: BioMarin Pharmaceutical
summary: Built a durable franchise of rare-disease enzyme-replacement and gene therapies, becoming one of the most consistently successful rare-disease drug developers over more than two decades.
lesson: Focusing exclusively on ultra-rare diseases with little competition builds a durable, high-margin franchise that larger pharma companies avoid.

name: BioNTech
summary: German mRNA biotech partnered with Pfizer to develop the first FDA-authorized COVID-19 vaccine, transforming from an obscure oncology-focused startup into a global vaccine powerhouse.
lesson: Partnering with a large pharma company for manufacturing and distribution can convert deep platform science into global-scale impact almost immediately.

name: BlaBlaCar
summary: French long-distance carpooling marketplace connecting drivers and passengers for intercity trips that has scaled profitably across Europe, Latin America and India without the capital intensity of owned-fleet models.
lesson: Matching existing car trips instead of operating a vehicle fleet gave BlaBlaCar structurally lower capital needs than ride-hailing rivals.

name: Bloom Energy
summary: Fuel cell power generation company that went public in 2018 and has become a supplier of on-site clean power to data centers and enterprises, including major AI data center deals.
lesson: Persisting through nearly two decades of R&D paid off once demand for reliable, on-site low-carbon power surged with data center growth.

name: Bolt (Taxify)
summary: Estonian ride-hailing and micromobility company that expanded across Europe and Africa with a low-cost model, reaching a multi-billion-dollar valuation while remaining independent of Uber and Lyft.
lesson: Undercutting Uber on commission and pricing in secondary European and African markets Uber deprioritized created durable market share.

name: Bombas
summary: D2C sock and apparel brand built around a one-for-one donation model grew to hundreds of millions in annual revenue with minimal outside funding.
lesson: A strong mission-driven brand story combined with disciplined bootstrapped growth can outperform heavily funded rivals.

name: Booking.com
summary: Dutch hotel booking site grew, after being acquired by Priceline, into the world's largest online travel agency by gross bookings.
lesson: Disciplined, metrics-driven performance marketing can out-execute flashier competitors over the long run.

name: Boston Dynamics
summary: Legendary robotics company behind Spot and Atlas has changed ownership several times but consistently produced industry-leading mobile robots.
lesson: Advanced robotics R&D can sustain a company through multiple ownership changes if its core technology stays ahead of the field.

name: Boston Metal
summary: MIT spinout developing molten oxide electrolysis to produce steel without fossil fuels, backed by Breakthrough Energy Ventures, moving toward commercial-scale green steel plants.
lesson: Tackling one of the hardest-to-decarbonize industrial sectors attracted patient, mission-aligned capital willing to fund a long hardware timeline.

name: Brex
summary: Corporate card and spend-management platform that started by serving venture-backed startups and later expanded into enterprise finance automation for larger companies.
lesson: A willingness to re-segment upmarket, from startups to larger enterprises, kept Brex's growth going once the smaller-company market matured.

name: Bumble
summary: A dating app requiring women to message first differentiated itself on safety and control, growing into a public company under founder Whitney Wolfe Herd.
lesson: A clear values-based product differentiation can carve out share in a saturated category.

name: Bungie
summary: Bungie built Halo into a defining console franchise, spun out from Microsoft, then built Destiny into a live-service hit before Sony's acquisition.
lesson: A studio can successfully reinvent itself across multiple corporate ownership structures if its core creative talent stays intact.

name: Cabify
summary: Spain-founded ride-hailing company focused on Spanish and Latin American markets that has remained independent and profitable-trending by avoiding a head-on subsidy war with Uber in its core Spanish-speaking markets.
lesson: Concentrating on Spanish-language markets with strong local regulatory relationships let Cabify avoid the capital-burning global expansion race.

name: Carbon Engineering
summary: Canadian direct air capture technology company, backed early by Bill Gates, that was acquired by Occidental Petroleum in 2023 for over $1 billion to scale its DAC plants.
lesson: Partnering with an oil major for capital and deployment infrastructure gave a hard-tech DAC company a viable path to scale.

name: CarbonCure Technologies
summary: Injects captured CO2 into concrete during mixing to both reduce emissions and strengthen the material; backed by Amazon's Climate Pledge Fund and deployed at hundreds of concrete plants.
lesson: A retrofit technology that improves an existing product's performance while cutting emissions removes adoption friction for a conservative industry.

name: Care/of
summary: Personalized vitamin subscription D2C brand was acquired by Bayer in 2020 for roughly $225M, validating its data-driven approach to supplement recommendations.
lesson: Personalization backed by a quiz-driven data model can differentiate a subscription commerce brand enough to attract a strategic buyer.

name: Careem
summary: Dubai-based ride-hailing 'super app' that dominated the Middle East and was acquired by Uber in 2019 for $3.1 billion, one of the largest tech acquisitions in the region's history.
lesson: Deep localization for regional payment and cultural norms let Careem out-compete Uber locally before Uber simply bought it.

name: Carvana
summary: Online used car retailer known for its car vending machines IPO'd in 2017, nearly went bankrupt in 2022-2023 under heavy debt, then executed a dramatic operational turnaround to become profitable.
lesson: A near-death balance sheet crisis can still be reversed with disciplined operational restructuring if the core demand is real.

name: Casetext
summary: AI-powered legal research platform, creator of the CoCounsel AI legal assistant, acquired by Thomson Reuters in 2023 for approximately $650 million.
lesson: Building genuinely differentiated AI tooling for a well-defined professional workflow can produce a strong strategic exit.

name: Cerebras Systems
summary: Maker of the wafer-scale engine, the largest single AI training chip in the industry, has continued raising large funding rounds and signing major cloud and government AI compute contracts.
lesson: A genuinely novel architecture can carve out a real niche in AI training even against Nvidia's dominance, if backed by patient capital.

name: Charm Industrial
summary: Converts crop waste into bio-oil injected underground for durable carbon removal, and has signed large offtake deals with Microsoft, Stripe, and JPMorgan.
lesson: Focusing on a measurable, geologically durable removal pathway helped win credibility with sophisticated corporate carbon removal buyers.

name: Checkr
summary: Background-check and identity-verification technology company for employers and gig platforms reached a multibillion-dollar valuation.
lesson: Modernizing background-check infrastructure with an API-first approach let Checkr ride the growth of the gig economy without being a gig platform itself.

name: Chewy
summary: Online pet supplies retailer built a loyal customer base through customer service and autoship subscriptions, was acquired by PetSmart for $3.35B in 2017, and IPO'd in 2019.
lesson: Deep category focus and exceptional customer service can beat Amazon even in a category Amazon also sells.

name: Chime
summary: The largest US neobank by account holders, offering fee-free checking and early paycheck access to primarily low- and middle-income customers; filed for a public offering in 2025.
lesson: Monetizing through interchange on everyday debit spending, rather than fees, aligned Chime's incentives with underserved customers' interests.

name: Circle
summary: Issuer of the USDC stablecoin built one of the most widely used dollar-backed tokens and completed its long-delayed IPO on the NYSE in June 2025.
lesson: A conservative, fully-reserved stablecoin model built patiently outlasted flashier algorithmic competitors.

name: CLEAR Secure
summary: Biometric identity verification company best known for expedited airport security lanes, expanded into stadium and venue entry and health verification.
lesson: A subscription identity-verification network gets more valuable as it expands into new physical venues.

name: Climeworks
summary: Swiss direct air capture pioneer operating the Orca and Mammoth DAC plants in Iceland, the largest operating DAC facilities in the world, with long-term offtake deals from Microsoft and others.
lesson: Being first to operate DAC at meaningful scale let Climeworks capture premium corporate removal contracts.

name: Clio
summary: Cloud-based legal practice management software for small and mid-size law firms became one of legal tech's largest unicorns.
lesson: Focusing relentlessly on the operational needs of an underserved segment built durable category leadership.

name: Coinbase
summary: Became the largest US crypto exchange and went public via direct listing on Nasdaq in April 2021, surviving multiple crypto winters through diversified revenue.
lesson: Investing early in regulatory compliance and trust can be a durable competitive moat in crypto.

name: Confluent
summary: Commercial company behind Apache Kafka, the dominant event-streaming platform, that went public in 2021 by building managed cloud and enterprise features around the open-source core.
lesson: Founders of a foundational open-source project are well-positioned to build the leading commercial cloud service around it.

name: Coupa
summary: Cloud procurement and business spend management software company went public in 2016 and was acquired by Thoma Bravo in 2023 for roughly $8 billion.
lesson: Procurement software with strong network effects across suppliers and buyers can command premium acquisition multiples.

name: Coursera
summary: Online course platform founded by Andrew Ng and Daphne Koller partnered with universities to offer courses and degrees; went public in 2021 and remains a leading global online learning platform.
lesson: Partnering with prestigious universities for credible content created durable brand trust at scale.

name: CRISPR Therapeutics
summary: Co-developed Casgevy with Vertex, the first CRISPR gene-editing therapy ever approved by the FDA (2023), for sickle cell disease and beta thalassemia.
lesson: Founding a company around a Nobel Prize-winning technology can deliver the first approved product in that class within a decade.

name: CrowdStrike
summary: Cloud-native endpoint security platform replaced legacy signature-based antivirus with a lightweight agent and cloud analytics, going public in 2019.
lesson: A cloud-native architecture can leapfrog legacy incumbents stuck with on-premise designs.

name: Crunchyroll
summary: Started as a fan-driven anime streaming site, Crunchyroll became the dominant global anime platform and was consolidated with Funimation under Sony.
lesson: Deep focus on an underserved but passionate fan community can build a defensible market position major studios eventually pay a premium to own.

name: Databricks
summary: Unified data and AI platform built on Apache Spark by its original creators, reaching a valuation of over $60B by riding both the big-data and generative-AI infrastructure waves.
